1: The righteous man perishes, and no one lays it to heart; devout men are taken away, while no one understands that the righteous man is taken away from calamity. The paradox of the righteous death: the community does not perceive that the taking-away of the righteous is actually merciful removal from coming judgment. The righteous perishing-without-being-understood is the shape of the Servant's death: he was led like a sheep to the slaughter, and as a sheep before its shearers is silent, so he did not open his mouth — in humiliation his justice was denied (Acts 8:32-33, citing Isaiah 53:7-8). The no-one-understands the righteous man's death in 57:1 is the same incomprehension that surrounds the Servant's death — after his suffering he will see light (Isaiah 53:11) but no one sees that his removal was for our healing.
2: He enters into peace; they rest in their beds, each who walked in his uprightness. The upright man's death is an entry into peace (shalom) and rest (menucha). The Servant who was denied peace in his humiliation (Isaiah 53:8: by oppression and judgment he was taken away) enters the peace of the vindicated righteous after death. In the NT: blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from now on; they will rest from their labor (Revelation 14:13). The rest-in-their-beds of the righteous departed is the theological category for the faithful dead who have entered their Sabbath rest (Hebrews 4:9-10: there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God; anyone who enters God's rest also rests from their own work). The shalom-entry at death anticipates the resurrection's fullness.
3: But you, come here, you children of a sorceress, you offspring of an adulterer and a prostitute. The oracle pivots to the idolaters: the sorceress-children are the covenant community that has pursued the occult religious practices of the Canaanites. The offspring-of-an-adulterer is the covenant-faithlessness language of Hosea — spiritual adultery as the category for idol-worship. In the NT: you adulterous people, don't you know that friendship with the world means enmity against God? (James 4:4). The children of the sorceress are the NT's children of the devil (John 8:44: you belong to your father, the devil) — defined by what/whom they serve rather than by ethnic origin.
4: Who are you mocking? At whom do you sneer and stick out your tongue? Are you not children of transgression, offspring of deceit? The mocking-and-sneering is directed at the righteous who suffer (vv 1-2) — the idolaters mock the very ones whose death is entered into peace. The children-of-transgression are defined by their parentage in sin: they mock the righteous because they are the offspring of deception. In the NT, the same dynamic is the crowd mocking Jesus at Golgotha: he trusts in God; let God rescue him now if he wants him, for he said I am the Son of God (Matthew 27:43). The mocking of the dying righteous is the historical expression of the spiritual mockery that Isaiah 57:4 diagnoses.
5: You who burn with lust among the oaks, under every green tree, who slaughter children in the valleys, under the clefts of the rocks. The specific idolatry indicted: the fertility-cult rites under trees (Baal-worship, with sexual and child-sacrifice components) are the covenant violations that define the sorceress-children. The slaughter-of-children in valleys (likely the Valley of Hinnom, Gehenna — 2 Kings 23:10, Jeremiah 7:31) is the most extreme covenant violation. In the NT: Jesus's warnings about Gehenna (Matthew 5:22, 29-30, Isaiah 10:28, Isaiah 23:33) are the eschatological judgment-language derived from this valley — the place of child-sacrifice becomes the image for the final judgment of those who sacrificed the innocent.
6: Among the smooth stones of the valley is your portion; they, they are your lot; to them you have poured out a drink offering, you have brought a grain offering. Shall I relent concerning these things? The smooth-stones-as-portion is the ironic assignment: the idolater who chose smooth stones as gods receives smooth stones as their inheritance. The grain offerings and drink offerings poured out to stones are the inverted worship that belongs to YHWH alone. The shall-I-relent rhetorical question implies the answer: no, not without repentance. In the NT: what a person sows, they will also reap (Galatians 6:7). The portion-you-chose is the portion-you-receive — the assignment of inheritance mirrors the object of ultimate loyalty.
7: On a high and lofty mountain you have set your bed, and there you went up to offer sacrifice. The high-mountain bed is the Canaanite high place of fertility-cult worship — the sexual-cultic imagery of the bed on the mountain is the covenant-faithlessness expressed physically. YHWH is the one who dwells on the high and holy mountain (v 15); the idolater's setting-a-bed-on-the-mountain is the parody-worship that inverts YHWH's genuine dwelling. In the NT, the distinction between the mountain of YHWH (Zion, the new Jerusalem, Hebrews 12:22) and the mountains of idolatrous worship is resolved: true worshipers will worship the Father in Spirit and truth, neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem (John 4:21-24).
8: Behind your door and doorpost you have set up your memorial; forsaking me, you uncovered your bed; you made it wide; you made a covenant with them; you loved their bed; you looked on their nakedness. The memorial behind the door-post inverts the mezuzah (Deuteronomy 6:9 — write YHWH's commands on your doorposts): instead of covenant commands, the idol-memorial marks the home. The covenant-with-lovers is the classic Hosea-Ezekiel language of spiritual adultery (Ezek 16, Hos 2). In the NT, the household devoted to YHWH is the church (1 Timothy 3:15: the household of God), and the faithfulness required in the household is the chastity of loyalty to one master: no one can serve two masters (Matthew 6:24).
9: You journeyed to Molech with oil and multiplied your perfumes; you sent your envoys far off, and sent them down even to Sheol. The extreme extent of the idolaters' pursuit: they go as far as Sheol (the realm of the dead) in their devotion to Molech. The going-down-to-Sheol is both literal (the child-sacrifice rites associated with Molech are a descent into death) and spiritual (the direction of all idol-devotion is downward, toward death). In the NT: the wages of sin is death (Romans 6:23). The journeying-to-Sheol for Molech is the directional opposite of the Servant's descent-to-Sheol on behalf of others — Christ descended into the lower parts of the earth (Ephesians 4:9) not to worship death but to conquer it.
10: You were wearied with the length of your way, but you did not say, It is hopeless; you found new strength, and so you did not grow faint. The relentlessness of idolatry: even when exhausted by the effort, the idolater finds new energy to continue. The not-saying-it-is-hopeless is the stubborn persistence of the devoted-to-the-wrong-thing. This is the inverse of YHWH's servant who will not grow faint or be discouraged (Isaiah 42:4). In the NT: those who run after other gods find no satisfaction (cf. the Prodigal Son's persistence in the far country before coming to himself, Luke 15:17). The energy for idol-pursuit that could have been directed toward YHWH is the theological waste that the gospel addresses: let us run with perseverance the race marked out for us (Hebrews 12:1) — but in the right direction.
11: Whom did you dread and fear, so that you lied, and did not remember me, did not lay it to heart? Have I not held my peace, even for a long time, and you do not fear me? The diagnosis of the root cause: not remembering YHWH drove the idolatry. The long-divine-silence was misread as YHWH's absence or indifference. In the NT, the apparent silence of God during suffering is the test of faith — Jesus cries My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? (Matthew 27:46), but the silence is not abandonment: you who fear him, trust in him — for he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one; he has not hidden his face from him but has listened to his cry for help (Psalms 22:24). The silence of YHWH is not his absence but his providence.
12: I will reveal your righteousness and your deeds, but they will not benefit you. The exposure of the idolater's so-called righteousness is the judgment of the divine court: your deeds-done-in-worship-of-idols will be held up and will be shown to profit nothing. In the NT: each one's work will become clear; the Day will bring it to light; it will be revealed by fire, and the fire will test the quality of each person's work (1 Corinthians 3:13). The works that cannot endure the light-of-judgment are the equivalents of the idol-works that YHWH promises to reveal as empty.
13: When you cry out, let your collection of idols rescue you! The wind will carry them all off; a breath will take them away. But whoever takes refuge in me will inherit the land and will possess my holy mountain. The contrast is absolute: the wind-blown-idols versus the refuge-in-YHWH that inherits the land. The whoever-takes-refuge has the same force as the gospel's whoever believes: the inheritance is available to all who trust in YHWH and is denied to those who trust in manufactured things. In the NT: whoever believes in him will not perish but will have eternal life (John 3:16) — the inheritance-of-the-land is the NT's eternal-life inheritance (Matthew 5:5: the meek will inherit the earth). The wind that blows away the idols cannot blow away what YHWH establishes.
14: And it shall be said: Build up, build up, prepare the way; remove every obstruction from my people's way. The highway-preparation oracle echoes Isaiah 40:3 (prepare the way of YHWH in the wilderness) and Isaiah 62:10. The removal of obstructions from the people's way is the work of the precursor — in the NT, John the Baptist (Matthew 3:3: prepare the way of the Lord; make straight paths for him). But the ultimate obstacle-remover is the Servant himself: the sin and guilt that blocked humanity's access to YHWH are removed by the atonement of Isaiah 53. The way-cleared-for-the-people is the new-and-living way opened through the veil, that is his body (Hebrews 10:20).
15: For this is what the high and exalted One says — he who lives forever, whose name is holy: I live in a high and holy place, but also with the one who is contrite and lowly in spirit, to revive the spirit of the lowly and to revive the heart of the contrite. The double-dwelling of YHWH — high-and-holy and contrite-and-lowly — is the theological climax of the chapter and one of the most Christologically significant verses in Isaiah. YHWH who lives in the high and holy place also dwells with the lowly: this is the condescension of the divine nature. Jesus is the incarnate form of this double-dwelling: the one who was in the form of God emptied himself, taking the form of a servant (Philippians 2:6-7) — the high-and-exalted-one who lived in the lowest place. The blessed-are-the-poor-in-spirit (Matthew 5:3) and the learn-from-me, for I am gentle and humble in heart (Matthew 11:29) are the Christological fulfillment of YHWH's dwelling-with-the-contrite-and-lowly. God opposes the proud and gives grace to the humble (James 4:6, 1 Peter 5:5, citing Proverbs 3:34): the principle of Isaiah 57:15 applied as ethical instruction to the NT community.
16: For I will not contend forever, nor will I always be angry, for the spirit would grow faint before me — the breath of life that I made. The limit of divine anger: YHWH's anger has a built-in mercy-limit because the spirit of man would expire if YHWH's contention had no end. The breath-of-life made by YHWH (Genesis 2:7: YHWH breathed into his nostrils the breath of life) is too fragile to sustain infinite divine wrath. In the NT: God did not appoint us to suffer wrath but to receive salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ (1 Thessalonians 5:9). The I-will-not-contend-forever is the theological ground for the atonement: if YHWH's anger were eternal and absolute against all sinners, there would be no possibility of covenant restoration. The finite-anger that makes space for mercy is the prerequisite for the gospel's rescue.
17: Because of the sin of his covetousness I was angry; I struck him; I hid my face and was angry, but he went on backsliding in the way of his own heart. The divine discipline that did not produce repentance: I struck him — the exile, the affliction — but he continued in his own way. The hiding-of-the-face is the covenant-withdrawal of Isaiah 54:8, here described as consequent to sin. The continuing-in-one's-own-way after discipline is the stubbornness that 48:4 diagnosed as bronze-foreheaded. In the NT, the same stubborn-persistence-despite-discipline is addressed in Hebrews 12:5-11: do not despise the Lord's discipline — it is for your good, so that you may share in his holiness.
18: I have seen his ways, but I will heal him; I will lead him and restore comfort to him and to his mourners. The divine initiative in healing: YHWH has seen the continuing backsliding (v 17) and chooses to heal anyway — the same logic as Isaiah 43:25 (I blot out your sins for my own sake). The I-will-heal is the Servant's mission explicitly: he was pierced for our transgressions; by his wounds we are healed (Isaiah 53:5). The healing, leading, and comforting — three dimensions of restoration — are the work of the Spirit (John 14:16-17: the Paraclete who comforts; the Spirit who leads you into all truth; the healing of the new heart). The mourners who receive comfort are the community of Matthew 5:4: blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted.
19: Creating the fruit of the lips. Peace, peace, to those far and near, says YHWH, and I will heal him. Paul applies this verse directly to the crucified Christ in Ephesians 2:17: he came and preached peace to you who were far away and peace to those who were near. The peace-to-those-far-and-near in Isaiah 57:19 was understood in Judaism as the inclusion of Gentiles (those far away) alongside Jews (those near): YHWH's shalom extends beyond the covenant community to the nations. Paul's argument in Ephesians 2:14-18 is that Christ himself is our peace, who has made the two groups one and has destroyed the barrier, the dividing wall of hostility — and has come and preached peace to those far and near (quoting v 19 exactly). The fruit-of-the-lips is the gospel proclamation through which this peace is announced: the lips that confess Christ as Lord make real the peace that Isaiah 57:19 promises.
20: But the wicked are like the tossing sea, which cannot rest, whose waves cast up mire and mud. The no-rest of the wicked contrasts with the entering-into-peace of the righteous (v 2) and the peace-peace to those near and far (v 19): the wicked have neither the rest of the righteous dead nor the shalom of the reconciled. The tossing sea is the primordial chaos-water of Genesis 1:2 that YHWH ordered at creation — the wicked inhabit the un-creation of moral chaos. In the NT: the sea gave up the dead that were in it (Revelation 20:13) and then the sea was no more (Revelation 21:1) — the tossing-sea of wickedness's final home is removed in the new creation. What the wicked inhabit is what YHWH's new creation abolishes.
21: There is no peace, says my God, for the wicked. The closing refrain (repeated verbatim from Isaiah 48:22) is the negative form of the chapter's positive theme: shalom is the inheritance of those who take refuge in YHWH (v 13) and the proclamation to those far and near who are reconciled through the Servant (v 19); the wicked who remain in their idolatry and rebellion have no access to this shalom. In the NT: there is no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:1) — the positive form of the promise; its negative: the cowardly, the unbelieving, the vile, the murderers, the sexually immoral, those who practice magic arts, the idolaters and all liars — they will be consigned to the fiery lake (Revelation 21:8). The no-peace-for-the-wicked is the final canonical word on the condition of the unreconciled.
